On this page

From Mott Scattering to the Rosenbluth Formula: Electron-Proton Elastic Scattering ​

1. Introduction: Why Protons Are Not Point Charges ​

The scattering of electrons by atomic nuclei was Rutherford's original tool for discovering the compact, positively charged core of the atom. In the non-relativistic limit, the differential cross section for a pointlike target is the famous Rutherford formula:

dσdΩ=α24E2sin4⁡(θ/2)

where E is the incident electron energy and θ the scattering angle. When the electron's kinetic energy becomes comparable to its rest mass, the formula generalizes to the Mott cross section, which incorporates relativistic spin effects via the replacement sin4⁡(θ/2)→sin4⁡(θ/2)⋅(1−v2sin2⁡(θ/2)).

But the proton is not a point charge. Deep inelastic scattering experiments revealed that the proton possesses internal structure—quarks and gluons bound by the strong interaction. Even in elastic scattering, where the proton remains intact, its finite size and magnetic moment modify the cross section in a manner that cannot be captured by a single coupling constant. The electromagnetic vertex of the proton must be described by form factors—functions of the momentum transfer that encode the spatial distribution of charge and magnetization within the proton.

The goal of this note is to derive the Rosenbluth formula, which expresses the electron-proton elastic scattering cross section in terms of two Sachs form factors, GE(Q2) and GM(Q2). These form factors are among the most precisely measured quantities in hadron physics and provide direct access to the proton's charge and magnetic radii.

2. Kinematics ​

Consider the elastic scattering process e−(k)+p(p)→e−(k′)+p(p′). The four-momenta satisfy:

k+p=k′+p′,q≡k−k′=p′−p

The Mandelstam variable t=q2 is negative for physical scattering. It is conventional to work with the positive quantity Q2≡−q2>0.

On-Shell Conditions ​

For elastic scattering, all external particles are on their mass shells:

k2=k′2=me2,p2=p′2=mp2

From p′=p+q, squaring gives:

mp2=mp2+2p⋅q+q2⟹p⋅q=−q22=Q22

Similarly, from k′=k−q:

me2=me2−2k⋅q+q2⟹k⋅q=q22=−Q22

Laboratory Frame ​

In the laboratory frame, the proton is initially at rest: p=(mp,0→). The incident and scattered electron momenta are:

k=(E,0,0,E),k′=(E′,E′sin⁡θ,0,E′cos⁡θ)

where we have taken the high-energy limit E≫me so that the electron is effectively massless. Energy-momentum conservation yields the relation between E′ and E:

q2=(k−k′)2=−2k⋅k′=−2EE′(1−cos⁡θ)=−4EE′sin2⁡θ2

and from p⋅q=Q2/2:

mp(E−E′)=Q22=2EE′sin2⁡θ2

Solving for E′:

E′=E1+2Empsin2⁡θ2

This relation is exact in the limit me→0 and will be essential for reducing the cross section to the Rosenbluth form.

3. The Electromagnetic Vertex and Form Factors ​

The Most General Lorentz-Covariant Vertex ​

For a pointlike Dirac particle of charge e, the electromagnetic vertex is simply −ieγμ. For the proton, which is a composite object, the vertex must respect Lorentz covariance, gauge invariance, and hermiticity. The most general form consistent with these constraints is:

Γμ=γμF1(q2)+iσμνqν2mpF2(q2)

where F1(q2) and F2(q2) are the Dirac and Pauli form factors, respectively. At zero momentum transfer:

  • F1(0)=1 ensures that the proton carries one unit of electric charge.
  • F2(0)=κp≈1.79 is the anomalous magnetic moment in units of the nuclear magneton.

The tensor σμν=i2[γμ,γν] couples the photon to the proton's magnetic dipole moment. The factors F1 and F2 are functions of q2 because the photon resolves the internal structure of the proton; at higher Q2, the photon wavelength becomes shorter and probes smaller distance scales within the proton.

Sachs Form Factors ​

It is often more intuitive to work with linear combinations of F1 and F2 known as the Sachs form factors:

GE(Q2)=F1(q2)−τF2(q2),GM(Q2)=F1(q2)+F2(q2)

where τ≡Q2/(4mp2)=−q2/(4mp2). The electric form factor GE reduces to the Fourier transform of the proton's charge density in the non-relativistic limit, while GM encodes the magnetic moment distribution. The Rosenbluth formula takes its simplest form when expressed in terms of GE and GM.

Gordon Decomposition ​

A useful identity, valid for on-shell spinors, is the Gordon decomposition:

u¯(p′)γμu(p)=u¯(p′)[pμ+p′μ2mp+iσμν(p′−p)ν2mp]u(p)

This shows that the Dirac current can be split into a convection part (proportional to the average momentum) and a spin part (proportional to the magnetic moment). While this decomposition is invaluable for physical interpretation, we will use the original form of Γμ for the trace calculations below, as it leads directly to the Sachs form factors.

4. The Scattering Amplitude ​

To lowest order in the electromagnetic coupling, the scattering process proceeds through the exchange of a single virtual photon. The invariant matrix element is:

M=(−ie)2u¯(k′)γμu(k)−igμνq2u¯(p′)Γνu(p)

or more compactly:

M=e2q2jeμ⋅jp,μ

with the electron current jeμ=u¯(k′)γμu(k) and the proton current jpμ=u¯(p′)Γμu(p). The factor 1/q2 is the photon propagator; the spacelike momentum transfer q2=−Q2<0 ensures that the interaction is mediated by a virtual photon.

The cross section requires |M|2, averaged over initial spins and summed over final spins. For unpolarized scattering:

|M|2―=e4q4LμνWμν

where the leptonic and hadronic tensors are defined by:

Lμν=12∑e-spinsu¯(k′)γμu(k)u¯(k)γνu(k′)=12Tr[(⧸k′+me)γμ(⧸k+me)γν]Wμν=12∑p-spinsu¯(p′)Γμu(p)u¯(p)Γνu(p′)=12Tr[(⧸p′+mp)Γμ(⧸p+mp)Γν]

The remainder of this note is devoted to the evaluation of these two tensors and their contraction.

5. The Leptonic Tensor ​

The leptonic tensor is evaluated using standard trace technology. Expanding the product:

Lμν=12Tr[⧸k′γμ⧸kγν+me⧸k′γμγν+meγμ⧸kγν+me2γμγν]

The traces of products with an odd number of gamma matrices vanish in four dimensions. Thus:

Tr[⧸k′γμγν]=kα′Tr[γαγμγν]=0,Tr[γμ⧸kγν]=0

For the trace of four gamma matrices, we use the identity:

Tr[γαγβγμγν]=4(gαβgμν−gαμgβν+gανgβμ)

Applying this to the first term:

12Tr[⧸k′γμ⧸kγν]=12⋅4(k′μkν−gμνk⋅k′+k′νkμ)=2(k′μkν+k′νkμ−gμνk⋅k′)

For the mass term:

me22Tr[γμγν]=me22⋅4gμν=2me2gμν

Collecting all contributions:

Lμν=2(k′μkν+k′νkμ−gμνk⋅k′+me2gμν)

In the high-energy limit E≫me, the electron mass can be neglected, and the leptonic tensor simplifies to:

Lμν≈2(k′μkν+k′νkμ−gμνk⋅k′)

This is the standard result for massless electrons and will be used in the contraction below.

6. The Hadronic Tensor ​

The hadronic tensor is the heart of the calculation. Its structure is constrained by two fundamental requirements: Lorentz covariance and electromagnetic current conservation. The latter, expressed as qμWμν=qνWμν=0, follows from gauge invariance and holds for any target, elastic or inelastic.

Parameterization by Symmetry ​

The most general symmetric rank-2 tensor that can be constructed from pμ and qμ and satisfies current conservation is:

Wμν=W1(−gμν+qμqνq2)+W2mp2(pμ−p⋅qq2qμ)(pν−p⋅qq2qν)

where W1 and W2 are scalar functions of q2 (or equivalently Q2). For elastic scattering, the on-shell condition p′=p+q implies 2p⋅q+q2=0, so p⋅q=−q2/2=Q2/2. The parameterization simplifies to:

Wμν=W1(−gμν+qμqνq2)+W24mp2(2pμ+qμ)(2pν+qν)

The task is now to determine W1 and W2 from the explicit trace formula.

Direct Trace Evaluation ​

Inserting Γμ=γμF1+iσμρqρ2mpF2 into the trace definition, we expand Wμν into three structures:

Wμν=F12⋅Tμν(1)+F1F2⋅Tμν(12)+F22⋅Tμν(2)

The F12 term is identical to the Dirac point-particle case:

Tμν(1)=12Tr[(⧸p′+mp)γμ(⧸p+mp)γν]

Evaluating the trace as in the leptonic case:

12Tr[⧸p′γμ⧸pγν]=2(pμ′pν+pν′pμ−gμνp⋅p′)mp22Tr[γμγν]=2mp2gμν

The odd terms vanish, giving:

Tμν(1)=2(pμ′pν+pν′pμ+gμν(mp2−p⋅p′))

Using p′=p+q and p⋅p′=mp2+p⋅q=mp2−q2/2:

Tμν(1)=2(2pμpν+pμqν+pνqμ+gμνq22)

The F1F2 cross term involves a single σ matrix. Using σνρ=i2[γν,γρ]:

Tμν(12)=12Tr[(⧸p′+mp)γμ(⧸p+mp)iσνρqρ2mp]+(μ↔ν)

The factor of iσνρqρ/(2mp) can be rewritten as −[γν,⧸q]/(4mp). Expanding the commutator and evaluating the trace with the standard identities yields:

Tμν(12)=−1mp(pμqν+pνqμ+q2gμν)

The F22 term involves two σ matrices. The product σμρσνσ can be reduced using:

σμρσνσ=−14[γμ,γρ][γν,γσ]

Taking the trace and contracting with qρqσ gives:

Tμν(2)=12mp2(qμqν−q2gμν)(p⋅q+mp2)=12mp2(qμqν−q2gμν)(mp2−q22)

Assembling the Full Hadronic Tensor ​

Combining the three contributions with their respective coefficients:

Wμν=2F12(2pμpν+pμqν+pνqμ+gμνq22)−F1F2mp(pμqν+pνqμ+q2gμν)+F222mp2(qμqν−q2gμν)(mp2−q22)

This expression must match the general parameterization. To determine W1 and W2, we compare the coefficients of the independent tensor structures gμν, pμpν, pμqν+pνqμ, and qμqν on both sides. After straightforward but lengthy algebra, one finds:

W1=−q24mp2(F1+F2)2=τGM2W2=(F1−τF2)2+τ(F1+F2)21+τ=GE2+τGM21+τ

where τ=−q2/(4mp2)=Q2/(4mp2).

A direct verification is provided by the limit F2=0, F1=1, which describes a pointlike Dirac proton. In this limit GE=GM=1, and the hadronic tensor reduces to:

Wμν(F2=0)=2(2pμpν+pμqν+pνqμ+gμνq22)

Substituting W1=τ and W2=1 into the parameterization gives exactly the same expression, confirming the consistency of the result.

7. Tensor Contraction ​

With Lμν and Wμν in hand, we evaluate the contraction LμνWμν. It is convenient to work in the massless electron limit and expand the contraction in terms of the parameterization coefficients W1 and W2.

Contraction with the Parameterized Form ​

Inserting Lμν=2(k′μkν+k′νkμ−gμνk⋅k′) and the parameterized Wμν, we exploit a key simplification: for massless electrons, the leptonic tensor is transverse to the photon momentum q:

qμLμν=qνLμν=0

This is the statement of electromagnetic current conservation (Ward identity) for the electron vertex. The proof is immediate:

qμLμν=2[(q⋅k′)kν+(q⋅k)k′ν−qν(k⋅k′)]

For massless electrons on shell, q⋅k′=(k−k′)⋅k′=k⋅k′ and q⋅k=(k−k′)⋅k=−k⋅k′ (using k2=k′2=0). Thus:

qμLμν=2[(k⋅k′)kν+(−k⋅k′)k′ν−qν(k⋅k′)]=2(k⋅k′)(k−k′−q)ν=0

since q=k−k′. Therefore, any term in Wμν proportional to qμ or qν gives zero upon contraction with Lμν.

Contribution from W1:

Lμν⋅W1(−gμν+qμqνq2)=−W1Lμμ+W1q2Lμνqμqν=−W1Lμμ

Computing the trace Lμμ=gμνLμν:

Lμμ=2(k′⋅k+k⋅k′−4k⋅k′)=−4k⋅k′

Thus the W1 contribution is:

LμνWμν(1)=4W1k⋅k′

Contribution from W2:

The W2 term contains (2pμ+qμ)(2pν+qν). Since qμLμν=0, the cross terms and the pure q term vanish:

Lμν(2pμ+qμ)(2pν+qν)=4pμpνLμν

Computing pμpνLμν:

pμpνLμν=2[2(p⋅k)(p⋅k′)−mp2(k⋅k′)]

In the lab frame, p=(mp,0→), so p⋅k=mpE and p⋅k′=mpE′. Using k⋅k′=EE′(1−cos⁡θ)=2EE′sin2⁡(θ/2):

pμpνLμν=2mp2EE′[2−2sin2⁡θ2]=4mp2EE′cos2⁡θ2

Thus the W2 contribution is:

LμνWμν(2)=W2mp2⋅4mp2EE′cos2⁡θ2=4W2EE′cos2⁡θ2

Total contraction:

Combining both contributions and using k⋅k′=2EE′sin2⁡(θ/2):

LμνWμν=4W1⋅2EE′sin2⁡θ2+4W2EE′cos2⁡θ2=4EE′[2W1sin2⁡θ2+W2cos2⁡θ2]

Substituting W1=τGM2 and W2=GE2+τGM21+τ:

LμνWμν=4EE′[2τGM2sin2⁡θ2+GE2+τGM21+τcos2⁡θ2]

This is the spin-averaged amplitude squared, from which the differential cross section follows by standard phase-space integration.

8. Phase Space and the Differential Cross Section ​

The unpolarized differential cross section for a 2→2 process is:

dσ=14(k⋅p)2−me2mp2|M|2―dΦ2

In the lab frame with me≈0, the flux factor reduces to:

4(k⋅p)2=4mpE

The two-body phase space in the lab frame is:

dΦ2=E′(4π)2mpdΩ=E′16π2mpdΩ

where dΩ=dcos⁡θdϕ is the solid angle element for the scattered electron. Using azimuthal symmetry (∫dϕ=2π for the unpolarized cross section):

dσdΩ=14mpE⋅e4q4⋅LμνWμν⋅E′16π2mp⋅2π

Simplifying with e2=4πα and q2=−4EE′sin2⁡(θ/2):

dσdΩ=α24E2sin4⁡(θ/2)E′E[GE2+τGM21+τcos2⁡θ2+2τGM2sin2⁡θ2]

This is the Rosenbluth formula. It is the relativistic generalization of the Rutherford and Mott formulas for a composite, magnetizable target.

9. Physical Discussion ​

The Pointlike Limit ​

For a structureless Dirac particle, GE=GM=1 independent of Q2. The Rosenbluth formula then reduces to:

dσdΩ|point=α24E2sin4⁡(θ/2)E′Ecos2⁡θ2

which is the Mott cross section for Coulomb scattering of a relativistic spin-1/2 particle. The factor cos2⁡(θ/2) is the helicity-flip suppression characteristic of magnetic scattering.

Separation of Form Factors ​

The Rosenbluth formula expresses the cross section as a linear combination of GE2 and GM2 at fixed Q2:

dσdΩ=A(Q2,θ)GM2(Q2)+B(Q2,θ)GE2(Q2)

By measuring the differential cross section at the same Q2 but different scattering angles θ (the so-called Rosenbluth separation), one can extract both form factors independently. This was the dominant experimental technique for proton form factor measurements for several decades.

At low Q2, the electric term dominates at forward angles (θ→0), where cos2⁡(θ/2)≈1 and sin2⁡(θ/2)≈0:

dσdΩ|θ→0≈α24E2sin4⁡(θ/2)E′EGE2(Q2)

At backward angles (θ→π), the magnetic term dominates:

dσdΩ|θ→π≈α24E2E′E⋅2τGM2(Q2)

Charge and Magnetic Radii ​

The slope of GE at Q2=0 determines the proton's charge radius:

⟨rE2⟩=−6dGE(Q2)dQ2|Q2=0

Similarly, the magnetic radius is extracted from GM. Precise measurements of these slopes have been central to the so-called "proton radius puzzle," where spectroscopic determinations of the charge radius disagreed with scattering extractions at the 4% level—a discrepancy that took nearly a decade to resolve.

Beyond Rosenbluth ​

The Rosenbluth separation becomes increasingly difficult at high Q2 because the GE2 term is suppressed by τ=Q2/(4mp2) relative to the GM2 term. For Q2≫mp2, the cross section is dominated by magnetic scattering, and GE becomes hard to extract. Polarization transfer measurements, where the recoil proton's polarization is detected, provide a more direct separation of GE and GM at high Q2 and have largely superseded the Rosenbluth method in modern experiments.

Vortex Electrons as a Probe ​

All of the preceding analysis rests on the plane-wave approximation: the incoming and outgoing electrons are treated as momentum eigenstates with definite four-momenta k and k′. In practice, accelerator beams are wave packets, and recent advances in phase-shaping technology make it possible to prepare electrons in Laguerre-Gaussian (LG) vortex states that carry intrinsic orbital angular momentum (OAM). Replacing the plane wave with a vortex wave packet changes the kinematics in a controlled way and opens new observables.

1. LG Wave Packets in Momentum Space ​

A paraxial LG electron state is characterized by an OAM quantum number ℓ, a radial index p, a beam waist w, and a transverse impact parameter b relative to the target. In momentum space its transverse profile is

ψℓp(k⊥;b)=w2p!π(p+|ℓ|)!(k⊥w)|ℓ|Lp|ℓ|(k⊥2w2)e−k⊥2w2/2eiℓϕke−ik⊥⋅b,

where Lp|ℓ| is the associated Laguerre polynomial. The prefactor ensures the normalization

∫0∞dk⊥k⊥∫02πdϕk|ψℓp(k⊥;b)|2=1.

The plane-wave limit is recovered when ℓ=0, p=0, w→∞ and b→0. In that limit the Gaussian becomes a delta function: e−k⊥2w2/2/π/w→δ(2)(k⊥), fixing the transverse momentum to zero.

The probability density |ψℓp|2 is independent of ϕk and of the phase factor e−ik⊥⋅b; it depends only on the modulus k⊥:

|ψℓp(k⊥)|2=w2p!π(p+|ℓ|)!(k⊥w)2|ℓ|[Lp|ℓ|(k⊥2w2)]2e−k⊥2w2.

For the fundamental mode (ℓ=0,p=0) this reduces to a simple Gaussian,

|ψ00(k⊥)|2=w2πe−k⊥2w2,

while for ℓ≠0 the distribution develops a ring-like structure with a zero at k⊥=0 and a peak at k⊥∼|ℓ|/w.

2. From Plane Waves to Vortex Cross Sections ​

Because the initial electron is no longer a momentum eigenstate, the scattering amplitude must be averaged over the transverse momentum distribution. The transition matrix element for a vortex state is the coherent superposition of plane-wave amplitudes weighted by ψℓp:

Mvortex=∫d2k⊥d2k⊥′ψℓp∗(k⊥′;b′)ψℓp(k⊥;b)Mpw(k,k′).

Squaring and summing over spins gives the transverse-momentum-resolved distribution

dσdΩd2P⊥=∫d2k⊥d2k⊥′δ(2)(P⊥−k⊥+k⊥′)|ψℓp(k⊥;b)|2|ψℓ′p′(k⊥′;b′)|2(dσdΩ)R.

Here P⊥ is the total final transverse momentum, which for plane waves is fixed to zero by momentum conservation. For vortex states it becomes a continuous variable. The presence of the impact-parameter phases e±ik⊥⋅b in ψ and ψ∗ means that even when the moduli |ψ|2 are azimuthally symmetric, the overlap integral carries b-dependent interference terms.

3. The b=0 Limit ​

When the beam axis is aligned with the target (b=b′=0), the angular integrations decouple and the cross section simplifies. Introducing the Fourier conjugate variable ρ for P⊥, the delta function can be written as

δ(2)(P⊥−k⊥+k⊥′)=∫d2ρ(2π)2eiρ⋅(P⊥−k⊥+k⊥′).

For b=0 the phase factors vanish and the azimuthal integrals over ϕk and ϕk′ give Bessel functions J0(k⊥ρ) and J0(k⊥′ρ). The result is

dσdΩd2P⊥|b=0=∫0∞dρρJ0(P⊥ρ)W~ℓp(ρ)W~ℓ′p′(ρ)(dσdΩ)R,

where W~ℓp(ρ) is the Hankel transform of the radial profile |ψℓp(k⊥)|2. For the fundamental mode (ℓ=p=0) this transform can be evaluated in closed form:

W~00(ρ)=∫0∞dk⊥k⊥J0(k⊥ρ)|ψ00(k⊥)|2=12πw2e−ρ2/(4w2).

The P⊥ distribution is then the autocorrelation of this Gaussian:

dσdΩd2P⊥|b=0ℓ=p=0=14πw2e−P⊥2w2/2(dσdΩ)R.

This is a Gaussian of width ΔP⊥∼1/w centered at P⊥=0. Integrating over P⊥ recovers the plane-wave Rosenbluth cross section, as required by normalization.

4. Corrections to the Form Factor Extraction ​

When the beam is tightly focused (w not much larger than 1/Q), the convolution with the LG profile samples a range of Q2 values around the nominal value. Expanding the Rosenbluth cross section to first order in the transverse momentum transfer,

(dσdΩ)R(Q2+δQ2)≈(dσdΩ)R(Q2)+δQ2∂∂Q2(dσdΩ)R,

where δQ2≈−2k⊥⋅k⊥′ at leading order in the small transverse momenta. Averaging over the LG distribution gives a correction proportional to the second moment of the transverse momentum:

⟨k⊥2⟩=∫d2k⊥k⊥2|ψℓp(k⊥)|2=2p+|ℓ|+1w2.

The corrected vortex cross section at b=0 therefore reads

dσdΩ|vortex=(dσdΩ)R+2p+|ℓ|+1w2Δ(Q2)+O(w−4),

where Δ(Q2) is a combination of GE, GM and their derivatives that follows from differentiating the Rosenbluth formula. For the fundamental mode this reduces to

Δ(Q2)=1w2[−11+τ(GEdGEdQ2+τGMdGMdQ2)cos2⁡θ2−2τGMdGMdQ2sin2⁡θ2].

This correction is of order (Qw)−2 relative to the leading term. For typical Jefferson Lab kinematics with Q∼1GeV and beam waists w∼10fm, the correction is at the percent level and could become competitive with the precision needed for the proton radius extraction.

5. The Impact-Parameter Modulation (b≠0) ​

The phase factor e−ik⊥⋅b is the origin of the most distinctive vortex signature. Restoring this factor, the overlap integral acquires a position-dependent phase that cannot be absorbed into the modulus. Writing b=b(cos⁡ϕb,sin⁡ϕb) and performing the angular integrals, one finds

dσdΩd2P⊥=∫0∞dρρJ0(P⊥ρ)W~ℓp(ρ;b)W~ℓ′p′(ρ;b′)(dσdΩ)R,

but now the transformed profile is complex:

W~ℓp(ρ;b)=∫0∞dk⊥k⊥J0(k⊥ρ)|ψℓp(k⊥)|2J0(k⊥b).

The appearance of J0(k⊥b) — the Bessel function encoding the geometric phase — modulates the P⊥ distribution. For the fundamental mode the integral can again be done in closed form:

W~00(ρ;b)=12πw2exp(−ρ2+b24w2)I0(ρb2w2),

where I0 is the modified Bessel function. The resulting P⊥ distribution is no longer a simple Gaussian. It develops an azimuthal dependence when b is comparable to w, with the asymmetry pattern rotating as ϕb is scanned.

Crucially, this b-dependent modulation is sensitive to the relative phase between the electric and magnetic terms in the Rosenbluth amplitude. If one writes the amplitude as

M∝GEME+GMMM,

then the cross section contains an interference term GEGMRe(MEMM∗). The geometric phase from b≠0 rotates this interference term in the complex plane, producing a ϕb-dependent modulation of the P⊥ spectrum. Measuring this modulation would provide direct access to the phase of the form factor ratio GE/GM, something that is extremely difficult to extract from plane-wave Rosenbluth data alone.

6. Experimental Prospects ​

In the limit w≫1/Q and b≪w, the vortex cross section collapses back to the Rosenbluth formula convolved with a narrow Gaussian in P⊥. The deviations discussed above become significant only when the transverse coherence length of the beam is comparable to the inverse momentum transfer.

For typical electron-scattering facilities this requires beam waists on the order of femtometers. Current phase-shaping technology can produce electron vortices with OAM up to ℓ∼100 and waists w∼10nm in the low-energy regime. Extending these techniques to the GeV energies needed for Rosenbluth kinematics remains a formidable challenge, but the theoretical framework is now in place: once such beams become available, the P⊥-resolved cross section will carry simultaneous information about the beam geometry and the proton's electromagnetic structure.

References ​

  • An Introduction to Quantum Field Theory, Ch. 6: Electron-Proton Scattering and the Rosenbluth Formula
  • Y. Yang and I. P. Ivanov, "Universal features of high-energy scattering of Laguerre-Gaussian states," arXiv:2604.00575 [hep-ph] (2026).
